data.title: Perion Network: Solid Quarter, Constructive Outlook - Buy
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Perion Network Ltd. or "Perion" (NASDAQ:PERI), previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Earlier this week, Perion Network reported better-than-expected third quarter results, with year-over-year growth in both display and search advertising:

Following a disappointing Connected TV ("CTV") performance last quarter, the channel rebounded with an impressive 75% year-over-year sales growth:

While web advertising sales were down both on a quarter-over-quarter and year-over-year basis, this was mostly a result of the company's recent decision to exit certain lower margin activities, as outlined by management on the conference call.

Operating cash flow of $5.9 million was impacted by net unfavorable working capital movements. However, management guided for a full-year cash flow conversion rate of 70% which should translate into decent cash generation in Q4.

Perion finished the quarter with $315.6 million in cash and liquid investments, down slightly on a sequential basis due to ongoing share repurchases, as discussed in more detail by management on the conference call:

During the quarter, we continued to execute our share buyback program. Due to regulatory volume restrictions, our repurchase activity was limited, leading to the repurchase of 800,000 shares for a total amount of $7.5 million.

As of September 30, 2025, we have repurchased a cumulative total of 10.4 million shares for $94.2 million.

This continued buyback commitment underscored our confidence in Perion's long-term value proposition.

As of today, during the fourth quarter, we already purchased an additional 1 million shares, and we expect to complete the current plan by the end of this year.

The board of directors also approved a $75 million expansion of the company's share repurchase program:

Looking ahead, Perion reaffirmed previously provided full-year expectations:

For 2026, management expects to scale the company's recently established Perion One platform:

We are expanding Perion One across more channels, deepening adoption with global brands and increasing recurring high-margin revenue streams, powered by AI, automation and self-service capabilities.

Each step brings us closer to our vision: a single intelligent platform that delivers better performance, faster decisions and long-term value for marketers and shareholders.

During the questions-and-answers session, some analysts appeared to be disappointed by management's decision to stick with full-year guidance despite another quarter of operational outperformance.

One analyst outright asked if current consensus growth expectations for 2026 might be too high, but the CFO pointed to quite the opposite:

(...) we actually believe that we will be able to beat next year as well the Street expectation. And our growth engines will continue to outperform the market, and I believe that it will drive our growth going forward.

And we are -- as much as we are focusing on the efficiency and we start to see this in terms of the numbers starting already for this Q, we are also investing in our current business to allow us to scale and to grow much faster also for next year.

Should the company manage to close 2025 on a strong note and provide initial guidance ahead of rather muted expectations, I would expect shares to reach new recovery highs following last year's search advertising disaster.

My unchanged price target of $14.75 remains based on a targeted 2026 EV/Adjusted EBITDA multiple of 5x, which represents a more than 60% discount to the U.S. advertising industry.

However, it will take more time and strong execution for the valuation gap to narrow after recent missteps.

As a result, I am reiterating my Buy rating on Perion Network's common shares.

Risk Factors

As usual, the bull thesis is not without risks. Should the company underperform growth expectations in the final quarter of the year and/or provide underwhelming 2026 guidance, the stock would likely face renewed selling pressure.

Bottom Line

Perion Network reported better-than-expected third quarter results, driven by an impressive rebound in CTV revenues.

The company continues to buy back shares in the open market and recently expanded its share repurchase authorization by $75 million.

While Perion only reaffirmed full-year guidance, management projected 2026 growth to exceed current consensus expectations.

Following last year's search advertising disaster, the company continues to trade at a steep discount to the broader industry. However, it will require more time and continued execution for the valuation gap to narrow.

At this point, I am reiterating my Buy rating on Perion Network's shares, with an unchanged price target of $14.75.

data.title: Meta: Why The Recent Selloff Is A Buying Opportunity (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Is Higher Spending Always a Good Thing?

Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) took a big hit after releasing Q3 FY2025 results. The selloff wiped out 50% of the entire rally since April 4th. Is the market overreacting? First of all, META recorded a $16 billion one-time expense in Q3 due to the President Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act" (which created a $6.2 diluted EPS headwind). This caused a big EPS miss, but it was not the reason for the selloff. Many companies recorded similar expenses in their recent quarters.

I believe the real reason is about META's aggressive tone on its AI spending outlook. In my last analysis, I maintained a Buy rating, because previous AI investment cycles have clearly drive strong growth for the company. The expanded AI capabilities have integrated with its family of apps, driving persistent +20% growth in advertising revenue. I also noted that this aggressive AI spending will continue this year, with capex as a percentage of total revenue expected to rise to 36% in FY2025, up from 23% in FY2024.

Now, we are seeing even more aggressive capex in FY2026, which is concerning. First, it will pressure margins, earnings, and FCF in the near-term, lowering the stock's quality. Second, these capex takes time to monetize and largely depend on management's execution, especially as the market worries about potential economic slowdown.

After a 19% post-earnings selloff, the stock's valuation has reset. I know that META's long-term GenAI roadmap requires higher upfront investments, deserving some risk premium (which means a higher discount rate and lower valuation), but I would also argue that the stock is already trading at a low multiple, with 21.4x non-GAAP P/E FY2026, assuming a flat YoY EPS growth. Therefore, the dip is worth buying, so I'm upgrading META to Strong Buy.

Q3 FY2025: Don't Focus Only on Revenue Growth

META's total revenue beat the high end of guidance every quarter this year including 3Q. Advertising revenue was up 25.6% YoY, showing continued acceleration from 21.5% YoY in the previous quarter. Without the one-time, non-cash charge, META should have topped EPS by a wide margin.

However, investors are not focused on META's revenue. The stock sold off because they are worried about the bottom-line, which I will discuss in the next section.

For Q4, META guided revenue to a range of $56 to $59 billion (including a 1% currency tailwind), which appears little light. The midpoint implies 19.9% YoY growth, as indicated on the chart above. Based on the recent quarters, my base case is that 4Q revenue could come in near $59 billion (implying a 22% YoY growth).

During the Q3 FY2025 earnings call, management said average price per ad jumped 10% YoY, driven by stronger advertiser demand and improved monetization. They also saw demand pulled forward for Quest headsets ahead of the holiday season.

Q3 Margin Contraction Driven by Higher R&D Costs

For earnings, Q3 diluted EPS would have been $7.25 per share excluding the tax charge. This suggests a 20.1% YoY growth. So why did the bottom line slow when revenue accelerated? That could be one of the key reasons why investors dumped META's shares. The company operating margin contracted by 200 bps on a sequential basis.

Looking at the table, R&D as a percentage of total revenue reached a record high, up by 300 bps QoQ while other expense segments remained stable. During the earnings call, the CFO said that compensation costs jumped significantly due to hiring more AI talent and higher infrastructure-related operating costs. This is what investors are nervous about.

FY2026 Will Be a Year of "Inefficiency"

META has been very committed to AI investments over the past few years, and management continues to raise the FY2025 capex outlook every quarter. In Q3, they raised the low end of the full-year capex range by $4 billion, now expecting $70-72 billion.

Q3 capex continued accelerating to 110.5% YoY, up from 100.8% YoY in Q2. Capex as a percentage of total revenue reached a new high of 37.8%. If we consider a $71 billions capex outlook (the midpoint), this suggests a 41% YoY growth in 4Q FY2025. This number still looks strong, especially when it compares to an 87.8% YoY in Q4 FY2024.

Even if FY2025 capex growth is expected to increase 81% YoY, management continues to pump even more money into AI investment in FY2026. In the earnings call, they guided that "capex dollar growth will be notably larger in 2026 than 2025" and "total expenses will grow at a significantly faster percentage rate in 2026."

I believe the market is expecting a big drop in margins in FY2026, and total revenue growth will likely normalize compared to this year (slowing on YoY basis). This could cause a sharp slowdown in earnings. All of these shows a deterioration in fundamentals. However, META needs to expand its AI infrastructure capacity to win over the long-term, but investors are worried about overspending in the wrong macro environment, which is a risk we can't ignore.

FCF Profile Is Ugly and Going to be Uglier

The strong capex will cause a big drop in FCF this year. So far, we have seen a continued decline in FCF over the past three quarters, and the YoY decline is getting bigger. FCF was down 31.5% YoY in Q3. Given more aggressive capex outlook in FY2026, we will likely see further FCF declines, which lower the stock's quality. However, after this major pullback, its valuation has already priced in a higher risk premium.

No Valuation Bubble

The GAAP P/E trades at 27.7x, nearly unchanged from 28x since my last rating four months ago. This means the stock's multiple hasn't expanded at all. On a non-GAAP basis, the P/E FY2025 is around 21.4x, adjusting for Q3 tax charge. Assuming a flat EPS over the next 12 months, trading at a 21.4x non-GAAP P/E FY2026 looks very attractive to me.

META is now trading at a lower P/E than its 5-year average of 22.9x. It's also significantly lower than GOOGL's 27.7x. META is the cheapest stock among the "Magnificent 7," which is why I believe the recent selloff is a strong buying opportunity.

Conclusion

In summary, META's recent selloff is overdone. It's fair for investors to worry about higher capex in FY2026, since larger cost structure will pressure margins, earnings, and FCF, which can lower the stock's near-term quality. But, META's long-term goal is beyond the advertising market. If META wants to maintain its current growth momentum and build top-tier GenAI leadership, it has to keep spending on AI. Of course, some investors may still be skeptical about the risk of overspending, but META's ROI from previous cycles has been strong. Given trading at 21x forward P/E now, I believe the risk and reward profile is very attractive.

data.title: Oakmark International Small Cap Strategy Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance highlights

Contributors

  • Wynn Macau (OTCPK:WYNMF)
  • Azimut Holding (OTCPK:AZIHF)
  • JDE Peet's (OTCPK:JDEPF)

Detractors

  • Amplifon (OTCPK:AMFPF)
  • LANXESS (OTCPK:LNXSF)
  • Hays (OTCPK:HAYPF)

Market Environment

International equities finished higher during the quarter, with 10 of 11 GICS sectors posting positive returns. By sector, financial and industrials contributed the most to market returns while consumer staples was the sole detractor. By country, Japan and Canada contributed the most to market performance, while Denmark and Germany detracted.

Portfolio Performance

The portfolio’s return was 0.97% (net) for the reporting period. This compares to the MSCI World ex USA Small Cap Index that returned 7.24% for the same period.

Top contributors:

Wynn Macau was a contributor during the quarter. The Hong Kong-based casino operator saw its stock price rise despite delivering underwhelming second-quarter 2025 results. This performance was supported by a strengthening Macau market in recent months, which has benefited Wynn as one of just six licensed operators in the city—and among the most efficient. We are encouraged by the company’s near-term plans to introduce updated rooms and a revamped Chairman’s Club, which should bolster its position in the premium segment. Wynn remains well-positioned for continued outperformance.

Azimut Holdings was a contributor during the quarter. The Italian financials firm’s stock price rose as it posted solid results during the period, in our view. We continue to appreciate management's focus on long-term value creation. We think the company remains strongly positioned, as it could be a beneficiary of strong macroeconomic tailwinds.

JDE Peet’s was a contributor during the quarter. The Netherlands-headquartered coffee company’s stock price steamed ahead after announcing that it would be acquired by American beverage giant Keurig Dr Pepper in January 2026. Keurig Dr Pepper agreed to purchase JDE Peet’s for EUR 31.85 per share plus an interim dividend, shy of our estimated fair value but a solid premium over its share price at the time. We believe there is a high probability that the deal will go through, as the largest shareholder will vote in favor of the transaction, and regulatory concerns are minimal. We have pared back our JDE Peet’s position in recognition of its recent gains. Still, we remain invested in the company due to its strong market position in what we view as an attractive consumer packaged goods segment.

Top detractors:

Amplifon was a detractor during the quarter. The Italian hearing aid company’s stock price plunged after first half results revealed that revenue grew more slowly than expected, and the company reduced its fiscal year guidance. While this news was disappointing, we believe this shortfall is due to market and cyclical issues in the U.S. and several European markets rather than a structural slowing of hearing aid demand. We appreciate the front-footed management team which launched a new cost program to offset the impact from the revenue shortfall. We also believe that Amplifon’s strong market position and ability to earn attractive profits remains intact. As such, Amplifon remains an attractive holding in our view.

Lanxess was a detractor during the quarter. The German-headquartered global specialty chemicals company’s stock price declined after it delivered results. The company decreased its full-year profit guidance, citing weakened demand and a challenging environment. Still, we see Lanxess as a well-managed company with a long runway for future growth.

Hays was a detractor during the quarter. The U.K.-headquartered human resources and recruitment firm’s stock price declined due to broader market weakness and uncertainty. Still, the company remains well-positioned in markets that we find attractive. Over time, we think management can help the company bolster its profitability as conditions become more favorable.

Portfolio Positioning

We initiated the following position(s) during the period:

Alten (OTCPK:ABLGF) is one of the world’s largest engineering service providers, partnering with companies to help them design, execute, and manage project-based engineering and IT services. Over time, R&D intensity is rising as a percentage of GDP as corporations invest in new technologies and capabilities to differentiate their products, accelerate the speed of product development, and comply with increasing regulation. To help manage this cost, corporations are increasingly outsourcing their R&D spending, making outsourced R&D services a GDP+ growth business through-cycle. Alten is one of a handful of companies in this space that is considered a global tier-1 service provider, which helps them tightly embed with customers in very sticky relationships. The business itself is asset light and very cash generative and is still run by its founder, Simon Azoulay, who we consider a shrewd capital allocator into bolt-on M&A that enhances the company's capabilities, customer list, and/or geographic reach. Alten’s stock price has contracted recently as the engineering services sector undergoes a cyclical downturn made worse by tariff uncertainty. We expect business fundamentals to eventually recover, driving an improvement in growth, profitability, and valuation, which is at historic lows.

Hamamatsu Photonics (OTCPK:HPHTF) is Japan's premier photon specialist, supplying detectors and light sources that enable complex machines to see what the human eye cannot and detect nanometer-scale defects. Hamamatsu is widely regarded as the preferred industry supplier and commands leading market share position across its major product categories. Moreover, the company's partnerships with its customers often begins in the concept stage of development, which has led to sticky, long-term relationships. In addition, we appreciate management's commitment to reinforcing its competitive moat through consistent reinvestment in research development, and believe it is poised to see an inflection point in its share price as cyclical headwinds subside. These headwinds provided us the opportunity to initiate a position in a dominant company with improving shareholder returns that is trading at a significant discount to our estimate of intrinsic value.

JEOL (OTC:JEOLY) manufactures, develops, and sells a range of scientific and industrial equipment. JEOL’s roots lie in its scientific instruments business, which sells tools like electron microscopes to academic institutions and food, chemical, and semiconductor customers, among others. Building and servicing this equipment requires significant scientific know-how, resulting in high barriers to entry and thus oligopolistic market structures. JEOL subsequently leveraged its electron beam technology to manufacture mask writing equipment, which fulfills an integral step in chip manufacturing that tethers the company to the world’s leading-edge foundries. JEOL services mask writers for years after selling them, creating a recurring revenue stream that helps smooth out the volatility of equipment sales. Additionally, management has intensified its focus on improving profitability in the scientific instruments business, where margins meaningfully lag global peers. In sum, we believe we are buying two good franchises – scientific instruments and mask writers – for the price of one.

Kansai Paint (OTCPK:KSANF) is one of the two market leaders in paint and coatings in Japan. The company’s products are used primarily for automobiles, construction, and ships along with bridges and residential housing. We have owned Kansai on and off over the past 15 years. We most recently sold out of the company in early 2024 as the share price reached our estimate of intrinsic value. Since then, the share price has declined more than 20% due to weak auto production and a weak decorative market in India as well as a de-rating of most global paint companies. Since then, management has focused on shifting emphasis to niches within the paint market, such as construction chemicals and wood coating while focusing less on retail. With the shares priced at more attractive levels, we reinitiated a position in the company. After speaking with management, we continue to believe that there is ample opportunity to reduce costs and improve profitability. Additionally, we believe many of the negatives of the Indian decorative market are now priced into the shares. Finally, we think management continues to allocate capital well and plans to return 100% of free cash flow through dividends and share repurchases.

Robertet (OTCPK:RBTEF) is a global leader in the production of flavors and fragrances and a near pure-play on the natural ingredients sub-segment. The company supplies premium components to high-end luxury brands such as Hermès, Chanel, and Aesop. Although these inputs represent a small portion of end products' cost of goods sold, they are critical to the consumer experience, defining the signature scent or taste and enabling Robertet to command premium pricing. Many of these scents and flavors underpin popular heritage products, leading to long-lived, recurring revenues. Despite its strong positioning, the stock trades at a discount to our estimate of intrinsic value due to cyclical headwinds and lingering investor overhang, which we believe are temporary. We appreciate Robertet's shareholder-aligned management team and believe their renewed efforts to expand, internationalize, and modernize the business will lead to significant value creation over the long term.

Aumovio (OTC:AMVOY) spun out of Continental in September 2025 and now develops, manufactures and distributes auto components and technology systems as a standalone company. Even without the backing of Continental’s tires business, there’s a lot to like about Aumovio. It built up strong positions in structurally attractive markets over the years under Continental. Aumovio operates through several segments that expose it to a compelling range of auto megatrends, like active safety, autonomous driving and software. Those trends have long teased upside, and we believe recent restructuring, portfolio optimization and a focus on returns will help Aumovio realize its potential. CEO Philipp von Hirschheydt and his management team deserve credit for that improved execution, and now that he’s armed with a cash-rich balance sheet following the spinoff, we expect his knack for underpromising and overdelivering to continue. We were excited to open a position in Aumovio at a price we believe is well below its intrinsic value.

We eliminated the following position(s) during the period:

  • BIPROGY
  • Hakuhodo DY Holdings (OTCPK:HKUOY)
  • Sapiens International (US Shs) (SPNS)

Outlook

For more than a decade, those of us who practice “value investing”—especially in overseas markets—witnessed U.S. growth/momentum stocks soar. We asserted that this trend was unsustainable as it was mostly driven by both weak foreign currencies as well as valuation expansion by U.S. growth stocks. Year-to-date, we are witnessing the unraveling of this paradigm. At the same time, the fundamentals of what we view as the most attractively priced global region, European equities, have improved thus driving more investor interest. As the valuation spread expanded during the last 10 years, our view was that the greater the spread was, the harder it would be to maintain it. We remain confident in our belief that given the large valuation spread that still exists, we will continue to benefit from this change.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. The gross performance presented above does not reflect the deduction of investment advisory fees. All returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains and the deduction of transaction costs. The client’s return will be reduced by the advisory fees and other expenses it may incur in the management of its account. The advisory fee, compounded over a period of years, will have an adverse effect on the value of the client’s portfolio.

Understanding the risks

All investments carry a certain degree of risk, including possible loss of principal. There is no assurance that an investment will provide positive performance over any time period. Foreign securities present risks that in some ways may be greater than investments in U.S. investments. Those risks include: currency fluctuation; different regulation, accounting standards, trading practices and levels of available information; generally higher transaction costs; and political risks. Value stocks may fall out of favor with investors and underperform growth stocks during given periods. Smaller companies' stocks often involve more risk than the stocks of larger companies. Stocks of small companies tend to be more volatile and have a smaller public market than stocks of larger companies. Small companies may have a shorter history of operations than larger companies, may not have as great an ability to raise additional capital and may have a less diversified product line, making them more susceptible to market pressure.

This material is not intended to be a recommendation or investment advice, does not constitute a solicitation to buy, sell or hold a security or an investment strategy, and is not provided in a fiduciary capacity. The information provided does not take into account the specific objectives or circumstances of any particular investor or suggest any specific course of action. Investment decisions should be made based on an investor’s objectives and circumstances and in consultation with his or her advisors.

The information, data, analyses, and opinions presented herein (including current investment themes, the portfolio managers’ research and investment process, and portfolio characteristics) are for informational purposes only and represent the investments and views of the portfolio managers and Harris Associates L.P. as of the date written and are subject to change without notice.

The specific securities identified and described in this report do not represent all the securities purchased, sold, or recommended to advisory clients. There is no assurance that any securities discussed herein will remain in an account’s portfolio at the time one receives this report or that securities sold have not been repurchased. It should not be assumed that any of the securities, transactions, or holdings discussed herein were or will prove to be profitable.

Glossary

The MSCI World ex USA Small Cap Index (Net) is designed to measure performance of small-cap stocks across 22 of 23 Developed Markets (excluding the United States). The index covers approximately 14% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in each country. This benchmark calculates reinvested dividends net of withholding taxes. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

The MSCI World ex USA Small Cap Value Index (Net) captures small cap securities exhibiting overall value style characteristics across 22 of 23 Developed Markets countries (excluding the United States). The value investment style characteristics for index construction are defined using three variables: book value-to-price, 12-month forward earnings-to-price, and dividend yield. The Total Return Index (Net) includes reinvested dividends net of foreign withholding tax. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

©2025 Harris Associates L.P. All rights reserved.

data.title: Considering The Fair Value Of Markets Amid Valuation Jitters
data.description:
With concerns about valuations, are there any pockets of the market that are still undervalued? David Sekera, Chief U.S. Market Strategist with Morningstar Research joins MoneyTalk to discuss how the fair value of markets has changed.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: A rising tide in the markets have been lifting stocks higher this year but with valuation concerns around some of those high-flying AI names, putting some jitters into the markets. Are there still any sectors that look undervalued? Joining us now to discuss, David Sekera, Chief US Market Strategist with Morningstar Research.

David, great to have you back on the program.

David Sekera: Hey, Greg, good to see you again.

Greg Bonnell: Let's start with those valuations, seems to be the big concern for investors right now trying to wrap their heads around it. Morningstar, I know they do fair value. We talk about it when you're on the program. It's changing their view on some of the big tech names.

David Sekera: Well, I too am trying to wrap my head around some of these numbers as well. Just the numbers we're talking about are just astronomical. So, for example, in just October, we increased our fair value on 170 different stocks under our coverage. Our increase has well outpaced our decreases by over a 3-to-1 ratio. And in fact, those valuation increases on the 6 largest stocks that we increased our fair values was $3.1 trillion worth of market cap.

Just to put that in perspective, think about it-- that's equal to adding 31 $100 billion companies to the total market valuation. And in the US, there's only 140 companies in the US Market Index with a market cap of over $100 billion. So our biggest increase was on Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL). In fact, we increased our fair value twice on Google over the course of the month. First was after they announced their partnership with Anthropic, and then second, after earnings came in better than what we expected. The total increase in market cap on Alphabet was $1.2 trillion. Now, to put that in perspective, that's larger than the entire market cap of Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A, BRK.B), which in and of itself is the 10th largest in the index.

We also increased our fair value on NVIDIA (NVDA) by $800 billion. That was after the CEO disclosed that they had over $500 billion worth of cumulative sales here in the end of calendar year 2025 and into 2026. That increase there, that's like adding the entire market capitalization of Walmart to the market index.

So, over the course of October, we had a total market increase of our valuations by $4.2 trillion. And, to some degree, it doesn't even look like it's over yet. We actually just increased our fair value on AMD yesterday following their investor meeting, as we incorporated much higher revenue guidance into our financial model.

Greg Bonnell: When you start to recalculate these valuations, what you consider to be fair value, is this catching up to the reality of the stock's moves already? Or do we still think there could be some more room to run in the space?

David Sekera: Well, there's a couple of different things going on. So, as far as whether or not there's still more room to run, that's going to be very idiosyncratic on each of the individual stocks. Now, the market perspective overall, I don't know-- it still feels like momentum wants to keep us moving higher. The market, according to a composite of our fair values, is still a couple percent undervalued. And I think Wall Street just wants to close out a very strong year with some very strong gains.

Longer term, I think investors really need to prepare themselves that from here, I think returns will be much closer to historically normalized types of returns-- essentially, a blended cost of equity over time. I think in 2026, be ready for bouts of volatility similar to what we saw at the beginning of 2025, just considering how much of a growth story all of these stocks are, considering that we are at pretty high valuations based on our growth expectations. Yeah, any type of negative headlines that could cause any doubt in the AI growth story could certainly put a lot of pressure on stocks in the short term.

But like I said, from an individual stock perspective, when we're looking at these big megacaps, those that we think are most closely tied to not only the AI buildout boom but also have very strong underlying fundamental businesses still look attractive, such as Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet. Some of the other ones where they're tied to AI, but the valuations in our mind already incorporate our growth or our base case for growth. NVIDIA, Amazon (AMZN), Broadcom (AVGO) are all still pretty fairly valued, whereas a stock like Apple (AAPL), we still think it's overvalued when we look at the growth trajectory, the company not necessarily really having a significant AI product out there yet. That's one that we think has probably shot too much to the upside.

Greg Bonnell: When you talk about potential for volatility, I've been having conversations saying that the market concentration, which we've been concerned about for a while, which obviously this is an issue that's not going away-- it's probably only getting more concentrated-- could mean even more volatile times ahead. What is happening out there with market concentration?

David Sekera: Exactly. Just with all of these fair value increases and the market rallying on these stocks, the big just keep getting bigger, and the concentration just continues to keep getting more and more concentrated. So, with those mega-cap stocks moving higher, larger percentage of the market-- if you look at the top 10 stocks in the Morningstar US Market Index, they're almost 40% of the total market capitalization. And when you look at those 10 stocks, 8 of them are all tied to AI.

And just to put some of this into perspective, if you look at NVIDIA back in 2020, that was only about 1% of the market capitalization. Now, that's up to 7% in and of itself. So, I just have to remind investors, even if you think you're pretty broadly diversified, you have a substantial amount of exposure in the indices-- which are very broad market indices-- you're still heavily exposed to AI-driven returns. It's been great for the upside the past couple of years. But as you noted, I do think it increases some of that downside risk if we have a sell-off in AI.

So, if you're an investor, and you're looking to reduce that concentration risk, or maybe you just have the lack of appetite for investing in AI at this point, yeah, I would recommend looking into diversifying into maybe the value space. Also look at small-cap stocks. Both of those are areas where we see a lot of attractive opportunities which are trading at discounts to our fair values. But they also have minimal exposure to artificial intelligence.

Greg Bonnell: So yeah, it's interesting, obviously, to take a look at other parts of the market that aren't getting a lot of attention right now. When it comes to the AI trade, I believe you're saying that next year we could see a pivot into people who have been building right now the AI infrastructure. But we're waiting for people to show us how they can utilize it.

David Sekera: Exactly. And I think we'd even talked about this a couple of months ago. I thought that that focus would have already shifted by now. But this year, it's still been all about the AI arms race, all about the buildout boom-- specifically just looking at the amount of growth that we've seen in hardware, data centers, and utilities.

But at some point, someone's got to be able to pay for all of this CapEx. And in order to do that, you need to have new economic value created by artificial intelligence. You need to have companies that will improve their existing products and services, companies that can develop new use cases and drive efficiency gains in order to be able to pay for these-- not even tens of billions, but hundreds of billions of dollars worth of CapEx being spent over the next couple of years.

So, I think next year, we're going to see much more focus identifying those companies that will benefit most from AI. Some of the individual areas that we're scouring and looking for these opportunities are in the software and services. I think that's the easiest area to see where AI can make those improvements. Seeing opportunities in health care and life sciences, research and development. I think the financial services industry will be able to use artificial intelligence to make some very large efficiency gains. E-commerce and social media already using AI. And then other areas, like transportation and logistics, I think, will be long-term beneficiaries.

Original Post

data.title: FrontView: Strategic Investment Can Lift Shares
data.description:
Shares of FrontView REIT, Inc. (FVR) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about a quarter of their value. In addition to ongoing concerns about commercial real estate in an elevated rate environment, shares have been weighed down by limited organic growth prospects as well as meaningful C-suite turmoil. Since IPOing in October 2024, the company has gone through two CFOs, creating meaningful uncertainty. However, FVR reported solid Q3 results and, more importantly, announced strategic actions, which may help to alleviate these concerns, making now a good time to review shares.

In the company’s third quarter, FrontView REIT generated $0.25 of FFO (funds from operations), which missed estimates by a penny. However, adjusted funds from operations (“AFFO”) was $0.32. AFFO is the metric FrontView guides to, and it excludes one-time and non-cash items, primarily tied to amortization of financing costs. AFFO excludes stock-based compensation, which is non-cash but a real expense in the form of dilution. Adding this expense back, my adjusted AFFO was $0.29, still a bit better than consensus. Rental revenue was $16.8 million, and base rent exited the quarter at an annualized ~$61 million level.

FrontView Has A Solid Asset Base

The company owns 307 properties with 2.6 million square feet of space. FrontView operates across most of the United States, with a presence in 37 states. Illinois is its largest exposure, at about 15% of base rent, followed by Georgia, Texas, Ohio, and Florida. Its primary exposure is restaurants at about 25% (split between quick service and casual), followed by medical & dental office space at 15% of base rent. Its exposure to apparel is less than 5% of rent. I view the lack of apparel exposure as a positive.

Importantly, sectors like medical offices and bank branches are much less economically sensitive, and its footprint overall has limited exposure to e-commerce-sensitive sectors. That said, restaurants do have more economic sensitivity, and companies like Chipotle (CMG) have flagged weakening traffic. Still, these chains have less sensitivity than higher-end, sit-down restaurants, and while 0%-5% changes in traffic can meaningfully impact shares of restaurant stocks, it is not likely to lead to material store closures or defaults on leases, which is the more relevant concern for investors in FrontView.

Additionally, its leases have a 7.2-year weighted average life. Its top 10 tenants account for about a quarter of its base rent, which is a fairly normal level of concentration in the industry. As you can see below, the majority of leases do not expire until 2031 or later, and even next year, just 5% of leases expire. 2027 will be a more meaningful year for renewal activity. It has been able to renew leases this year at a 4% higher rate, so expirations have actually been a net positive for the business. That is because there has been so little construction of retail space that prime outdoor locations have a limited supply, pushing up lease rates. I would also note that its leases have a 1.6% average annual increase. Almost all leases have a fixed annual increase, though a minimal fraction (~1%) are tied to CPI.

Growth Capacity Has Been Limited

Given the lack of supply, FrontView ended the quarter with 98% occupancy, up 20bps sequentially. Still, occupancy is down 90bps from last year, though I view the current level of occupancy as very strong and more normal, given a typical level of churn among tenants. I expect occupancy to be stable or tick up slightly in Q4, given the lack of renewals remaining this year.

During the quarter, it spent $16 million on acquisitions at a 7.5% cap rate. However, it sold more properties than it bought. Dispositions this year have been done at a 6.8% cap rate, with over 40% tied to the restaurant sector. With shares trading at closer to a ~9% cap rate, these dispositions are being done at a much higher multiple. In theory, if FVR could sell its whole portfolio at this cap rate, it would be worth over $17 a share. That makes these dispositions attractive.

These dispositions also help the company manage down its debt position. FVR has $309 million of debt, leaving it with 5.3x net debt to EBITDA leverage. I would ideally like to see leverage closer to 5x, though it can operate in the 5-5.5x area. Still, with leverage over 5x, there is limited room to grow given the finite debt capacity and its 67% dividend payout ratio. Actions taken subsequent to quarter end should help reduce leverage and facilitate growth.

Preferred Issuance Is A Major Positive

Alongside earnings, the company announced an agreement to sell $75 million of convertible preferred stock, which it will use to finance ~$100 million of 2026 acquisitions. The deal is “delayed draw,” meaning that FrontView does not need to accept the capital and begin paying the dividend right away. Instead, it will draw the capital as it needs it to fund M&A; however, it must be fully drawn within one year. The preferred will pay 6.75%, and owners have the right to convert into FVR stock at $17. FrontView can also redeem the preferred starting three years after the final draw of funds, and as part of this agreement, it will add a representative from Maewyn Capital to its board of directors.

This transaction represents fairly attractive financing for FrontView in my view. As an alternative to issuing preferred stock, the company could issue common equity to support M&A. Its stock currently yields 6.3%, so the preferred dividend cost is about 0.45% higher. However, shares are below $14, whereas these can only be converted at $17, meaning there will be ~20% less dilution with ~1.1 million fewer shares issued. Paying just a slightly higher dividend to reduce the potential dilution of this substantially is the right move.

Of course, while this is an attractive way to finance acquisitions, it will still be critical for the company to find sound properties to invest the proceeds in, where it can earn a return that exceeds this financing cost. Management expects to deploy funds at a 7.5%+ cap rate, which is consistent with where it has been transacting this year, making that a reasonable assumption in my view. Once fully deployed, we should see run-rate FFO 2%-3% higher.

To me, this announcement is a major positive, for several reasons. First, the cost of financing is fairly attractive and certainly preferable to issuing common stock. Second, it will allow FVR to pivot to a growth footing in an accretive way. Increasing scale may help FVR to gain focus among a wider set of investors and boost its valuations. Finally, this substantial strategic investment is a critical vote of confidence in a management team that has been beset with turnover, creating an overhang over shares, which should gradually dissipate.

Shares Are Attractive

Alongside results, the company tweaked up 2025 guidance, expecting FFO to be a penny higher while also increasing both investment and disposition plans. It is also providing “preliminary” 2026 guidance, targeting 3%-4% FFO growth and $100 million of M&A. I would note this growth is primarily due to ongoing lease increases, as we are unlikely to see more than a 1% benefit from its preferred issuance, given it will take time to close deals and for them to start contributing.

Once at run rate, FrontView should have at least $1.35 in AFFO capacity, giving shares a ~9.5% AFFO yield, even after their rally today. I believe this rally is merited, as the strategic investment helps to solve many of the firm’s problems. FrontView appears to be a hidden gem with an asset portfolio undervalued by investors, amid its small size and management turmoil. With a strategic investor in place, focus on its underlying, stable assets can help shares recover. In this space, I view a 7%-8% FFO yield as fair, or $17-$18. With over 20% upside, FrontView is a “Buy.”

data.title: Federal Reserve Cuts Less Certain And May Not Bolster Labor Market Anyway
data.description:
Markets have currently priced in further rate cuts coming in December, and while I believe we will indeed see further rate cuts, it’s worth noting that there is a growing divide on the Federal Open Market Committee, with inflation hawks becoming increasingly vocal. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell has also cautioned that further rate cuts are not a given. Powell has consistently emphasized data throughout his tenure, and I believe high COVID inflation has left a mark on his thinking. If the Federal Reserve does pause rate cuts, markets will likely sink as expectations adjust, and that might be only the beginning of the problem (more on this later).

A Federal Reserve Divided

Not everyone gets a vote on the FOMC, and several Federal Reserve Bank presidents only get votes on a rotational basis. At the moment, it appears that voting members lean more dovish, while hawks remain in the wings. Cleveland President Beth Hammack and Dallas President Lorie Logan have both publicly expressed opposition to further cuts. However, neither has voting power right now. Kansas President Jeffrey Schmid voted against cuts in October and has a chance to vote again in December. It’s also quite possible that other voting members on FOMC actually would prefer to pause interest rate cuts but have been voting with Powell out of a sense of solidarity. A publicly divided FOMC is more likely to rattle markets.

The Federal Reserve hasn’t released minutes for the October FOMC meeting yet. The minutes can yield more insight into what members of the Committee are thinking and also help chart the overall outlook regarding the economy. In the September minutes, some participants argued that inflation would be about 2% even if tariffs were excluded. Members also noted hearing from business leaders that prices were likely to rise owing to increased input costs. A majority of participants also mentioned upside risks and the possibility that inflation could remain elevated even outside of the tariffs.

The important takeaway, by my estimates, is that the current FOMC members were by and large cautious even before the October meeting. The government shutdown has delayed job reports, but other indicators suggest a weakening labor market and persistent inflation. In September, most indications from voting members suggested that rate cuts in December were likely. While I believe we’ll see another rate cut in December, it does seem less certain than before.

If the Federal Reserve opts not to cut rates, I suspect the pushback will be severe. With Powell close to the end of his term, President Donald Trump may show restraint and keep things at a verbal level. Still, the President is already not a fan of Powell, and perhaps he might try to push him out of the Chair spot. This would almost certainly sink markets, although I find it unlikely to happen.

CME Group’s FedWatch tool now shows that the chances of a rate cut are just 53% in December. I’d put the odds higher, but a month ago, I’d have figured there was around an 85% chance of cuts. Now, I’d put it at 65%. I also worry that the rate cut may not stimulate job growth as much as hoped, and that could leave the Federal Reserve in an even more precarious position.

Will Rate Cuts Stimulate Enough Job Growth?

The Federal Reserve has a dual mandate to both guard against inflation and to support full employment. If the unemployment rate falls between 4 to 5%, it’s generally viewed as full employment, as there will be some people switching careers, and turbulence (like a company going out of business) can lead to temporary job loss. Generally and historically speaking, raising interest rates reduces inflation but can increase unemployment. Cutting interest rates can fuel inflation but should reduce the unemployment rate. At least, that’s how it’s generally viewed by many economists and Federal Reserve officials.

Larry Summers, a former Treasury Secretary, noted in an article back in 2016 that the recovery from the Great Recession didn’t unfold as expected. It was assumed that the contraction would be steep, but also that the recovery would be relatively quick. Theoretically, aggressive stimulus policies and low interest rates should have turbocharged the economy, and that was what many economists expected. Interest rates stayed near zero for several years, and yet high unemployment, as pictured below, persisted.

We’re a long time past the Great Recession, and while it took many years, the American economy, as well as many other economies, eventually did recover, and unemployment rates improved. Still, it’s fair to wonder if we could face a similar scenario today in which low interest rates may prove ineffective at improving unemployment. We don’t have Federal data for October yet, but Revelio Labs found that the economy shed 9,000 jobs in October, with retail, manufacturing, and government sectors shrinking considerably. Still, growth in healthcare services offset much of the decline. Goldman Sachs is even more pessimistic, noting that as many as 50,000 non-farm jobs may have been lost.

Keep in mind, this weakness comes even after a rate cut. Ideally, we’d have seen more strength in the labor market. Meanwhile, UPS, Amazon, Synopsis, Target, and other companies have announced sweeping layoffs even as consumer demand remains stable and amid prior rate cuts. In at least some cases, AI is being blamed for job loss, as is offshoring. If AI and offshoring are responsible for widespread job loss (a big but important “if”), low interest rates might actually exacerbate the issue by making it cheaper for businesses to invest in AI (and perhaps offshoring as well). Researchers previously found that low interest rates could encourage automation by making capital cheaper and encouraging a shift from investing in labor to investing in capital (including machinery). I can’t help but wonder if we could face scenarios in which low interest rates encourage companies to invest in AI or other projects that could reduce demand for labor.

What Will The Federal Reserve Look Like In 2026?

Jerome Powell will soon be vacating the Federal Reserve Chair position, and President Trump will have his chance to replace him. We know that the White House has a short list of five candidates: Federal Reserve governor Michelle Bowman, governor Christopher Waller, former governor Kevin Warsh, Trump Administration economic adviser Kevin Hassett, and BlackRock senior managing director Rick Rieder.

It’s widely assumed that once President Trump appoints a new chair that we’ll see more interest rate cuts. Yet it’s important to remember that the Chair only has one vote and cannot compel others to agree with rate cuts. Cleveland President Hammack and Dallas President Logan, who have voiced opposition to further rate cuts and are set to join FOMC as voting members in 2026. (Kansas President Schmid, who recently voted against rate cuts, won’t be on the committee in the new year.)

It’s hard to say what voting members on the committee will do if they at large disagree with rate cuts even if the Chair is pushing for them. Should committee members more or less openly rebel and oppose rate cuts, I suspect the market instability would be immensely high. And who knows how Administration officials might respond. Efforts to pressure FOMC members and governors not aligned with the Administration's vision could escalate.

Key Takeaway: December Cuts Far From Guaranteed And Future Turbulence Is A Major Risk

It’s hard to say what the future holds, but December rate cuts look more uncertain now than they did a few weeks ago. Even if a rate cut does occur, what happens in 2026 is still uncertain. Looking at the economic environment now, there are data points that inflation hawks and labor market hawks can both point to to justify their positions. As for December, if a rate cut doesn’t come, expect markets to take a hit.

data.title: BILL Holdings: Sale Rumors, Activist Pressure, And Why I'm Only Upgrading To Hold
data.description:
Introduction

Despite a recent stock rally and a Starboard stake reported in September, BILL Holdings, Inc. (BILL) has underperformed the index (SPY) since my Sell rating in March 2025.

In my last analysis, I flagged a few concerning trends in BILL’s financials, including decelerating growth, macroeconomic pressures, deteriorating take rates, competition, and inflated financial performance (SBC inflating cash flow). Despite these issues, BILL traded at a 120 P/E and 565 EV/EBITDA, which seemed unreasonably high. Via a discounted cash flow, or DCF, analysis, I ascertained that BILL was priced for ~10% CAGR and 21% FCF margin.

I listed the following as developments that could change my mind:

  • Stabilization in retention rates (>95% DBRN)
  • Improved take rate mix
  • Y/Y revenue growth sustained over 15%.

As I explore in the article that follows, while the fundamental bear case (growth + unit economics + competition) is stronger today than in March, the equity downside is less open-ended because governance and strategic dynamics have changed.

I’m going to cover three distinct insights tied to BILL’s latest numbers and yesterday’s sale rumors.

Recent Developments

To begin, BILL’s growth and retention have been better than my “spiral” case, even as the headline growth slows.

In its fiscal first quarter 2026 (Q1 FY26) report (ended September 30, 2025), BILL recorded total revenue of $395.7 million (up 10% Y/Y) and core revenue of $358 million (up 14% Y/Y). BILL guided its FY26 revenue for between $1.5965 billion and $1.6265 billion, implying ~9-11% total revenue growth versus FY25’s $1.4626 billion.

Recall that my bear case hinged on revenue decelerating toward high single digits. However, despite a subdued SMB macro backdrop, core revenue growth held steady in the mid-teens. Moreover, BILL’s net dollar retention rate was 94% (as of Q4 FY25).

Moving on, as I warned in my March analysis, unit economic pressure is now clearly visible in Divvy rewards and mix. BILL’s gross margin in FQ1 was 80.5%, compared to 82.0% in FQ1 last year.

In the Q1 FY26 earnings call, management stated they are now “scrutinizing and actively adjusting” the reward structure after Divvy card rewards increased to 132 bps of payment volume.

Meanwhile, BILL’s sales & marketing expense rose from 35% to 38% of revenue Y/Y in Q1 FY26 due to higher reward expense and marketing to promote the card and spend platform.

SBC still accounts for a high amount of FCF. For instance, in Q1 FY26, SBC of $59.2 million represented 72% of FCF ($82.3 million). While this was improved by over 78.5% in FY25, it is still unusually high. Palantir (PLTR), for instance, had SBC of ~$172 million in Q3 2025 on ~$500 million in FCF. So, SBC is a major caveat to consider when modeling BILL on a DCF basis. The company is having to spend nearly all its “profits” to retain top talent amid a very competitive market.

The biggest structural change since my last look is in governance and strategic options. BILL has strengthened its balance sheet and, in August 2025, announced a new $300 million share repurchase program, which it has since begun executing. These developments signal confidence from management in its ability to generate cash despite GAAP net income oscillations.

As for Starboard, it is pushing for improved performance and value creation. The activist investor has built an ~8% stake in BILL and added four independent directors to BILL’s thirteen.

Yesterday, a Truist analyst floated a sale value range of $65-85 per share, implying as much as 60% upside from the pre-rumor price range. This comes after a Bloomberg report that BILL is “working with a financial adviser to solicit interest from larger industry rivals as well as private equity firms.”

I would definitely view these developments with a “grain of salt.” Think about it. Larger companies aren’t reportedly reaching out to BILL. It is the opposite. While the analyst also pointed out that BILL is trading below the average multiple of deals in the fintech industry, there are good reasons for this (e.g., ~10% Y/Y growth is lower than fintech M&A comps when acquired, GAAP operating margin near breakeven, and so on).

Valuation Assumptions

What is BILL priced for?

To find out, I conducted a DCF model that isolates revenue growth (CAGR) and FCF margin.

As for inputs, “Year 1 Revenue” is BILL’s FY26 midpoint estimate. One quarter has passed (“Fraction of Year Elapsed”). My Base case assumes 10% CAGR and 20% FCF margins. However, given BILL’s high SBC, “FCF Margin” was adjusted to 12.5% to reflect SBC as 37.5% of FCF. This gives us the true earnings available to shareholders after accounting for dilution. Moving on, a “Beta” of 1.6 is blended and close to peers in the Software industry.

Most everything else is either standard DCF methodology (e.g., terminal growth of 2.5%) or was derived from BILL’s most recent financial statements (e.g., 10-Q).

Honestly, I think this is an optimistic model. BILL’s midpoint guidance for FY26 implies 10% growth. The Base case above assumes BILL can hold steady throughout the nine-year projection.

Moreover, as I pointed out earlier, SBC makes up nearly double what I projected in the Base case. While BILL is making marginal improvements there, its sluggish revenue growth and pressured margins are unlikely to make a big impact on SBC as a % of FCF.

As pictured above, SBC as 50% of FCF may be more realistic. It’s probably also more realistic to model BILL for a 7.5% CAGR (this represents the average throughout the nine-year projection) rather than what they are projected to do in just Year 1.

That said, one could also make a case for the Bull scenarios, as its “core growth” is in the low-teens.

Normalizing to exclude SBC (remember, the Base case calls for a 20% FCF margin), the assumptions in the Base case appear highly feasible.

As you can see in the trends above, the drop in FCF margin is quite apparent, and it is not associated with accelerating revenue growth.

Final Words

The engaged activist with board representation, combined with a formally explored sale process, does create a strategic backstop for BILL that wasn’t there in March.

Still, my original triggers for becoming optimistic (e.g., DBNR >95%, sustained 15%+ growth, and improved take-rate mix) haven’t been met.

While BILL appears undervalued with conservative assumptions on a cash flow basis, SBC is a major asterisk.

Altogether, I think I have enough reason to rerate BILL to Hold.

That said, I am not running to buy BILL because it is exploring an acquisition. Fundamentals are subtly deteriorating (I think this could become more apparent in its next two quarters as BILL's FQ1s benefit from strong seasonality), and BILL is unlikely to warrant a high premium if it actually convinces a suitor.

data.title: Why Silver's Bull Market Is Still Young
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

A few days ago, I published a popular report called “Why Gold’s Bull Market Is Still Young,” where I analyzed gold’s current secular bull market by comparing it to its previous two secular bull markets in the 1970s and the 2000s.

I examined how gold stacks up not only in U.S. dollars but also across a range of other yardsticks, including the U.S. Consumer Price Index, the M2 money supply, the national debt, and the Dow. The purpose was to show that gold’s bull market is still in its early stages, with significant gains still ahead.

Now, as promised, I’ve created a similar report focused on silver, using the same format and methodology. The findings indicate that silver’s bull market is still in its early stages, with significant potential for further gains. So let’s get started.

For the purposes of the exercises in this report, the dates I’m using for the two prior secular silver bull markets are November 1971 to January 1980 for the 1970s bull market, and November 2001 to April 2011 for the 2000s bull market. These time frames are widely accepted as the official start and end points of those respective bull markets.

As for the current secular silver bull market, while there is some subjectivity and debate around when it began, I’m using September 2022 as the starting point. I believe this is well justified based on where silver bottomed, both in dollar terms and relative to the other four yardsticks used in this report. This can be clearly seen in the charts I’ve included.

Let’s begin with the most rudimentary reference point: the spot price of silver in U.S. dollars. During the secular bull market of the 1970s, silver rose by 3,631% over 98 months. In the 2000s secular bull market, it gained 1,130% over 113 months.

By comparison, the current secular silver bull market is up only 200% over just 37 months. This is a clear indication that the current bull market is still in its early stages relative to the previous two, and I believe it has much further to run.

Next, let’s examine the real price of silver, which is silver adjusted for inflation using the U.S. Consumer Price Index (CPI). This is a more meaningful metric than the nominal price shown earlier because the key question is not how much silver has increased in absolute terms, but whether it is keeping up with inflation. After all, precious metals are the best hedges against inflation over the long run.

During the secular bull market of the 1970s, the real price of silver rose by 1,860% over 98 months. In the 2000s secular bull market, it gained 875% over 113 months.

In contrast, the current secular silver bull market is up only 177% over just 37 months. This further supports the view that the current bull market is still in its early stages compared to the previous two, and I believe it is just getting started.

Now we will look at silver in relation to another measure of inflation: the U.S. M2 money supply. The money supply may be an even better indicator of inflation than the CPI, which is known to understate actual inflation.

Moreover, growth in the money supply is the underlying cause of inflation itself. As Milton Friedman, the Nobel Prize–winning economist, famously said, “Inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon.”

During the secular bull market of the 1970s, silver measured against the M2 money supply rose by 1,670% over 98 months. In the 2000s secular bull market, it gained 634% over 113 months. In comparison, the current secular silver bull market is up only 195% over just 37 months.

This is yet another indication that the current bull market is still quite young, and those claiming it is long in the tooth are clearly out of touch with the objective facts.

Finally, we measure gold against the Dow Jones Industrial Average, which I find to be a particularly useful yardstick. There is a well-established principle that equities and precious metals move in opposing long-term cycles, where one outperforms the other as large amounts of capital rotate between them.

When major secular bull markets in stocks come to an end, such as in the early 1970s or early 2000s, a significant amount of capital typically flows out of equities and into precious metals, and I believe we are about to see that dynamic play out again, which will send gold and silver's current bull market into overdrive.

During the secular bull market of the 1970s, silver measured against the Dow rose by 3,745% over 98 months. In the 2000s secular bull market, it increased by 900% over 113 months. In comparison, the current secular silver bull market is up only 98% over just 37 months, which indicates that, by historical standards, it has barely even begun.

Now that we’ve looked at the various yardsticks in chart form, I’ve compiled all the data on silver’s performance in past and current secular bull markets into this helpful table to show how the current one compares.

As you can see, by every metric, the secular bull market that began in September 2022 still pales in comparison to the previous two. This is a clear indication that it has many more years and gains ahead.

For example, if silver were to match the performance of its 1970s bull market, it would reach a peak of $664 per ounce. If it were to replicate the performance of the 2000s bull market instead, it would peak at $219 per ounce. In both cases, these targets are significantly higher than the current price of $53.40 per ounce.

Despite silver’s impressive 200% price gain since September 2022, there is still very little participation from American investors, as evidenced by the largely unchanged silver holdings of the popular SLV ETF.

This indicates that silver’s bull market is far from being frothy or mature and has many more innings to go as American investors eventually pile in and drive it to much higher levels.

Another important factor to consider is that the yardsticks I used to compare the price of silver—the money supply, national debt, and the CPI/inflation—will continue to grow strongly and will even accelerate in the years ahead as we approach the fiscal and monetary endgame not just in the United States but around the world.

That factor alone will continue to push the nominal price of silver higher, even if it does not gain ground relative to those benchmarks, although I expect it will.

A return to quantitative easing (QE), which is essentially digital money printing, is only a matter of time. No government or central bank can resist using the printing press for very long if they have access to it, and that is the ultimate reason for owning precious metals.

The Fed’s quantitative tightening (QT), or shrinking of its balance sheet, since 2022 has set the stage for the growing financial stress now emerging, which is reminiscent of what occurred in late 2019 and ultimately led to massive QE that was officially attributed to the COVID pandemic.

Notably, at its October meeting, the Fed announced it is ending the drawdown of its still-substantial balance sheet, citing signs that money market liquidity conditions are tightening and bank reserve levels are falling.

The lack of gold backing of the U.S. dollar and all other major world currencies since 1971 opened the door to rampant money supply expansion, which is the root cause of inflation. The introduction of quantitative easing in 2008 accelerated this trend significantly.

As a result, the U.S. M2 money supply has surged nearly fivefold since the year 2000. This is the underlying cause of the severe cost-of-living crisis we face today, where many hard-working people can no longer afford a middle-class lifestyle.

I expect money supply growth to accelerate even further as we approach the fiscal and monetary endgame, which will be a tremendous boon for gold and silver prices.

As Milton Friedman, the Nobel Prize–winning economist, famously said, “Inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon.” It is no surprise, then, to see the most widely referenced measure of inflation, the Consumer Price Index (CPI), surge alongside the money supply.

In fact, the CPI has nearly doubled since the year 2000, placing immense pressure on the middle class and fueling a wave of radical political movements.

An increasing number of politicians are unfairly and incorrectly blaming capitalism for the soaring cost of living, rather than acknowledging the real issue: the abandonment of sound money backed by gold and silver.

Finally, we turn to the chart of the U.S. national debt, which has ballooned nearly sevenfold since the year 2000, reaching a staggering $38.15 trillion. It is now growing by an astonishing $1 trillion every 100 days.

This trajectory is clearly unsustainable, and we are heading toward a breaking point that will ultimately lead to the collapse of the paper dollar, euro, pound, yen, and all other fiat currencies. And when that happens, precious metals will be the big winner.

Wrapping things up, I hope I have made it clear that silver’s current secular bull market is still in its very early stages, just as I demonstrated with gold in my previous report.

Although the nominal price of silver may appear high and potentially discouraging to some, nominal prices can be misleading. This is why it is important to compare them against other yardsticks to assess whether a precious metal or commodity is truly expensive.

As the findings in this report show, silver remains quite undervalued relative to those measures. This should ease concerns that silver’s price is too high or that its bull market is nearing an end.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Enbridge: Quiet Stability Hiding Bigger Yield Potential By 2027
data.description:
Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) stock has declined almost 2% since I initiated coverage in September for the midstream company. On Nov. 7, the company reported its third quarter earnings. In this report, I assess Enbridge’s Q3 earnings and I update my price target for the stock.

Resilience In Liquid Pipelines, Growth In Natural Gas And Renewables

Enbridge’s Q3 earnings rose from C$4.2 billion to C$4.27 billion, or 1.6%, primarily driven by higher results in gas transmission and distribution and storage and renewable power generation as an emerging driver of growth. Liquid pipelines results were modestly lower, driven by lower contribution from the Gulf and Mid-Continent network. Distributable cash flow declined by C$30 million to C$2.566 billion, driven by higher financing costs and maintenance capital.

Year-to-date, adjusted EBITDA increased 9% to C$14.74 billion, driven by 61% growth in the Gas Distribution & Storage, driven by new projects, equity investments, and better contracting terms, and 16% growth on Gas Transmission & Midstream, driven by the acquisition of The East Ohio Gas Company in March last year. Liquid Pipeline results were stable year-on-year, while Renewable Power Generation results were lower, driven by weaker European wind results. Distributable cash flow increased 3.7% to $9.25 billion, which is lower than the growth in adjusted EBITDA due to higher financing costs, maintenance, and income taxes.

Third quarter results show the stability of the Liquid Pipelines business and the growth of the gas assets business, with renewables as an emerging growth driver. I believe that is exactly what makes Enbridge attractive.

Enbridge Reaffirms Guidance

The company reaffirmed its guidance for 2025 with adjusted EBITDA between $19.4 billion and $20 billion and expected to be in the upper end of the range while distributable free cash flow per share has been guided at $5.50-$5.90. Tailwinds this year include mainline volumes, the USD/CAD rate, and the 10% equity investment in Matterhorn Express offset by lower Gulf and Mid-Continent volumes and US interest rates. Higher interest rates are a headwind currently for the simple reason that the company added or refinanced much of its debt in a high-interest rate environment and only new debt will benefit from lower interest rates as most of the debt is fixed rate.

Results and Guidance Not Necessarily Exciting, Which Is Good

The results that Enbridge booked and the guidance are not necessarily exciting, which I believe is a good thing as it shows the stability of the business with stable growth prospects. Midstream companies have stability as a core foundation through extremely little exposure to volatility in commodity prices limited macroeconomic exposure through volume driven agreements, while many parts of the business operate on a take-or-pay basis where clients pay a fixed fee regardless of volume or commodity prices.

Lower interest rates will be good for the business overall, as the composite cost of debt will eventually come down as existing debt is being refinanced. We do note that initially, as interest rates come down, investors may have more appetite for higher-risk investments and that may cause some weakness in the stock price for Enbridge for the simple reason that it's not a high-risk name with predictable growth. However, this potential pressure is only transient because with lower interest rates investors will also look at better yield options, and that's also what Enbridge provides.

Enbridge Stock Remains Attractive

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I believe that Enbridge stock remains attractive even though my price target has been adjusted to $58.28 from $60.77, driven by a deeper level of modeling. Assumptions on EBITDA and free cash flow have barely changed. On EBITDA, estimates declined in the 0.3%-0.6% range for 2025-2027 for a total downward adjustment of 0.4%, while CAGR is expected to be around 5.5%. Free cash flow estimates have been lifted by 13% this year, but that's largely offset in the years beyond resulting in a total lift on free cash flow of 1.7%, and the CAGR flattened modestly from 12.8% to 12.4%.

EBITDA margins are expected to bottom this year and then tick up substantially in the years ahead. The more interesting story in my view is the year-on-year increases in operating cash flow. Currently free cash flow is expected to decline through 2026 as capital expenditures are elevated, but as capital expenditures tick down in 2027, we also see the quality that Enbridge is building in the business now. From a yield perspective, I do believe that buying now will drive the yield on costs to around 6% by 2027. Leverage is expected to tick down substantially once capex moderates. The company has a high leverage, but that's to be expected given the high capital cost for developing new energy infrastructure and is in no way worrisome given that the cash generation is predictable.

Conclusion: Enbridge Remains Attractive

I have reduced the price target slightly, but what we continue to see is that Enbridge has a consistent growth path that should pave the way for higher stock prices and increasing dividends. The year-on-year upside to the stock price does not necessarily make it attractive, but in combination with the current undervaluation I do believe Enbridge is definitely a buy.

data.title: Market Signals: November Global Equity Brief
data.description:
AI leadership shows few signs of slowing

Large-cap growth stocks extended their lead in October, powered by continued optimism around artificial intelligence. The MSCI World Index gained 2%, with the largest technology names again accounting for much of the advance. The spread between the smallest and largest stocks in the index was wide (again), underscoring concentrated leadership.

Looking ahead, we see AI investment likely broadening beyond the current leaders as enterprises expand adoption across sectors. Corporate commitments to hardware, cloud infrastructure, and data systems suggest the AI buildout is still in its early stages. This ongoing capital formation could continue to shape market dynamics into 2026, especially as companies seek to convert innovation spending into measurable productivity gains.

Is there an AI bubble? The evidence is still mixed

Speculation has naturally followed the extraordinary performance of AI-linked equities. While valuations are rich in several areas, earnings momentum continues to underpin investor confidence. The next phase may test the sustainability of this narrative - particularly as markets begin to differentiate between genuine innovators and those priced for perfection.

We believe AI-related spending is likely to remain a structural feature of corporate strategy in the tech sector, even if the pace normalizes. In the near future, AI investments will have to show meaningful returns on investment. At which point, profitability, scalability, and sustainable cash flows will determine which firms sustain leadership. In our view, this shift from enthusiasm to execution could define the next leg of the AI cycle - one where fundamentals, not headlines, drive performance.

Beyond AI: Slowing growth and a quality focus

Beyond the technology complex, we expect global growth to continue moderating into 2026. The labor market remains a key watchpoint, with forward indicators pointing to slower demand and cautious corporate investment. Inflation persistence is keeping central banks attentive, with policy calibration likely to remain a key variable for equity markets.

Against this backdrop, we see investors leaning toward high-quality companies with resilient earnings and conservative balance sheets. We believe portfolios are best positioned toward businesses with the ability to generate steady cash flows across cycles. As the macro environment cools, diversification across styles, sectors, and regions may help investors capture selective upside without overexposure to any single theme.

Investor implications

AI remains a defining force in global equities, and we believe its influence will extend well into the next market cycle, for better or worse. Extreme market concentration risk and evolving macro conditions call for balance. We believe investors may benefit from maintaining exposure to innovation while reinforcing portfolio resilience through diversification and quality. In our view, a forward-looking approach - anchored in fundamentals - offers the best path to participate in growth while preparing for change.

Important information pertaining to the hypothetical example: Past performance does not predict future returns. Return level is proportionately scaled in line with cash level to be overlaid. Source: Russell Investments. Assumptions: Average cash level 1.0%, 10-year history from 12/31/2023, gross of fees. Opportunity cost from not securitizing cash varies by asset allocation and time period, and is represented by horizontal bars as marked within the chart legend. Target asset allocation used: 0% cash, 74% MSCI World, 26% Global Aggregate (GBP Hedged). For illustrative purposes only. Does not represent any actual investment. Indexes are unmanaged and cannot be invested in directly. Performance benefit (net) of overlaying cash by last 5 individual calendar year is as follows: 2023:20 bps, 2022:-17bps, 2021:16bps, 2020:14bps, 2019:23bps.
